_comment: Worked example. Replace all values. Omit optional fields when conversation has no relevant content.

title.description: Short human-readable title for this topic

slug.description: Kebab-case, max ~40 chars, derived from title

summary.description: One paragraph: what was discussed and what was decided

decisions.description: Resolved decisions. Each entry is a plain-text statement. Empty array if none.

open_threads.description: Genuinely unresolved questions needing follow-up. Empty array if none.

next_action.description: The single most important next step. Not a list.

evidence.description: One or two sentences: data, complaints, or research that motivated this

further_notes.description: Miscellaneous notes that don't fit other fields

base_seed.description: Lineage. Path to the seed this one was merged from on a Mode 2 handoff return trip. Omit on fresh seeds.

title: Replace session tokens with short-lived JWTs
summary: Current auth stores opaque session tokens in Redis. Legal requires short-lived, auditable tokens. Decision: migrate to signed JWTs with 15-min expiry and a refresh-token rotation scheme. Redis session store is removed after migration.
decisions: Use signed JWTs (RS256) with 15-minute access token expiry
Refresh tokens stored in HttpOnly cookies, rotated on every use
Redis session store is deprecated and removed after migration, not alongside it
No silent refresh in the client — expired token triggers a full re-auth UX
open_threads: Token revocation strategy for compromised refresh tokens is unresolved
Whether to support multiple active sessions per user needs product sign-off
next_action: Run /to-tasks to generate the migration task breakdown
problem_statement: Opaque session tokens in Redis do not carry claims, cannot be audited externally, and have no expiry short of explicit revocation. Legal has flagged this for compliance.
solution: Replace session tokens with RS256-signed JWTs. Access tokens expire in 15 minutes. Refresh tokens rotate on use and are stored in HttpOnly cookies. Redis session store is removed post-migration.
evidence: Legal audit (2026-05-15) flagged session token storage as non-compliant with the new data residency policy. Three internal incidents involved stale tokens remaining valid after account deletion.
success_metrics: Access tokens expire within 15 minutes of issuance
Zero Redis session store reads in production after migration cutover
Refresh token rotation rate matches login rate (1:1) within 5% tolerance

user_stories.feature: inspect token claims without a Redis lookup
user_stories.benefit: so that auth state is self-contained and debuggable at the edge

user_stories.feature: audit token issuance timestamps in the JWT payload
user_stories.benefit: so that compliance reports can be generated without querying Redis

implementation_decisions: JWT signing uses RS256 with a 2048-bit key pair; private key stored in secrets manager
Refresh token table added to Postgres: columns id, user_id, token_hash, expires_at, revoked_at
Auth middleware validates JWT signature and expiry before any handler runs
Migration: dual-read period where both old session tokens and new JWTs are accepted
testing_decisions: Unit tests cover token signing, expiry validation, and refresh rotation logic
Integration tests hit the real Postgres refresh token table, not a mock
Regression test: stale session token rejected after cutover date
out_of_scope: Token introspection endpoint for third-party consumers
risks_and_tradeoffs: 15-minute expiry shortens the window for revocation but increases refresh round-trips
Dual-read migration period leaves old session tokens valid; any Redis breach during this window is still a risk
further_notes: RS256 key rotation cadence not yet decided — revisit before go-live
